skill.name: clickhouse-io
skill.description: ClickHouse database patterns, query optimization, analytics, and data engineering best practices for high-performance analytical workloads.
skill.summary: Apply ClickHouse schema, query, ingestion, and monitoring patterns for analytical workloads.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: The skill is a Markdown guide with SQL and TypeScript examples, not executable automation. Static command, filesystem, environment, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are mostly false positives from fenced examples, but one sample teaches unsafe SQL string construction with interpolated trade fields.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe SQL String Construction in Example
medium_findings.description: The bulk insert example builds VALUES text by interpolating trade fields into a query string. This is documentation content, but users could copy the pattern into production and expose ClickHouse ingestion code to SQL injection or malformed data issues.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected lines show values derived from trade fields placed directly inside query text. The risk is mitigated because the skill is documentation-only, but the pattern itself is unsafe to teach.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Fenced Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections correspond to Markdown code fences and SQL or TypeScript examples. I found no executable script, hook, or instruction that runs shell commands from the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are inside Markdown fences that demonstrate database usage. There is no package code path that executes these examples automatically.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Configuration Example
low_findings.description: The environment variable references show normal ClickHouse and PostgreSQL connection configuration in sample TypeScript. No evidence found of secret logging, network exfiltration, or hidden credential harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples access expected database connection settings. They do not transmit secrets beyond the illustrated database clients.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Import Is Non-Executable Documentation
low_findings.description: The filesystem detection is an import inside a streaming insert example. The imported API is not used to read local files or collect sensitive data in the shown content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access indicator is limited to a single import statement in a code fence. No file path, read operation, or persistence behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts appear to match database terminology, SQL ordering, Markdown text, and analytics examples. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, host reconnaissance, privilege escalation, or prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the surrounding content shows analytics guidance and SQL examples, not security-sensitive primitives. The line references do not contain actionable malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interpolated Database Query Template
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample ClickHouse insert constructs query text with interpolated record fields. Marketplace users should replace this pattern with parameterized inserts, validated serialization, or client-supported structured insert APIs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shown query combines dynamic values into SQL text. The evidence is direct, although the skill does not execute the code itself.

content.value_statement: ClickHouse schemas and analytics queries are hard to tune for large datasets. This skill provides practical patterns for tables, inserts, materialized views, monitoring, and analytics.
actual_capabilities: Explains MergeTree, ReplacingMergeTree, and AggregatingMergeTree table patterns.
Shows query patterns for filters, aggregations, windows, funnels, cohorts, and retention.
Documents batch ingestion, streaming ingestion, ETL, and CDC approaches.
Provides materialized view patterns for real-time aggregation workflows.
Lists monitoring queries for slow queries and table storage statistics.
Summarizes ClickHouse design practices for partitioning, ordering keys, and data types.
limitations: It is a static guide and does not connect to a ClickHouse server.
Examples need adaptation for each schema, client library, and deployment model.
Some insertion examples should be hardened before production use.
It does not cover cluster operations, backups, replication, or security administration.

use_cases.title: Design an Analytics Schema
use_cases.description: Choose table engines, partitions, ordering keys, and data types for event or market analytics data.

use_cases.title: Tune Expensive Queries
use_cases.description: Rewrite analytical queries to use indexed filters, ClickHouse aggregate functions, and efficient result ordering.

use_cases.title: Plan Reporting Pipelines
use_cases.description: Map ingestion, ETL, CDC, and materialized view patterns for near real-time dashboards.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Table Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clickhouse-io skill to design a ClickHouse table for [data type]. Include engine, partition key, ordering key, and data type choices.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clickhouse-io skill to review this ClickHouse query for performance. Explain better filters, aggregation functions, and ordering choices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Materialized Views
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clickhouse-io skill to design materialized views for [metric]. Include source table assumptions, aggregate states, and query patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Analytics Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clickhouse-io skill to assess this ingestion and reporting pipeline. Cover batch size, CDC flow, table design, monitoring, and production risks.

output_examples.input: Design a ClickHouse table for daily user events.
output_examples.output: A practical response would recommend a MergeTree table, monthly time partitions, an ordering key based on date and user identifiers, and compact data types.

output_examples.input: Review a slow dashboard query over trade events.
output_examples.output: A useful answer would prioritize indexed date filters, explicit selected columns, ClickHouse aggregate functions, and checks against the slow query log.

output_examples.input: Plan hourly metrics for a live analytics dashboard.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest an AggregatingMergeTree target table, a materialized view with aggregate states, and merge functions for dashboard queries.

best_practices: Start from the query patterns before choosing partitions and ordering keys.
Batch inserts and validate serialized values before loading production data.
Use monitoring queries to check slow queries, storage growth, and merge behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not copy interpolated SQL examples into production without safe parameter handling.
Do not use SELECT star for high-volume analytics queries.
Do not rely on small frequent inserts for sustained ClickHouse ingestion.

faq.question: Does this skill execute ClickHouse queries?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with examples and design patterns.

faq.question: Can it help choose a ClickHouse table engine?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares common MergeTree family patterns for analytics, deduplication, and pre-aggregation.

faq.question: Does it include query optimization guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers indexed filtering, aggregations, window functions, and common analytics query shapes.

faq.question: Is the sample insertion code production ready?
faq.answer: Not as written. Validate values and use safer client-supported insert methods before production use.

faq.question: Does it cover operational security?
faq.answer: No. It does not cover authentication, authorization, encryption, backups, or cluster hardening.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
